role_summary: Co-founder of the Atlanta Student Movement and co-author of the 1960 Appeal for Human Rights; later Atlanta NAACP chapter president.
ai_reading: King's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 144), the Mississippi SNCC organizer, with C. T. Vivian (entry 134, the SCLC figure who taught nonviolent direct action in the South) close behind at 133. The cluster reads King as a movement-bridge voice between the Atlanta sit-in tradition he co-founded and both the Mississippi voter-registration cohort and the SCLC direct-action school; the embedding does not pull him into a strict local-Atlanta or Atlanta-University-Center cluster the cultural framing of him would have predicted. One convergence is worth flagging: Amos C. Brown (entry 2) is both a top-five semantic neighbor by frequency (count 93) and the only interviewee whose testimony the influence graph records as discussing King, and King's own out-edges run to Reginald Robinson (entry 101), another of his high-count neighbors, so the cosine-similarity grouping and the who-discussed-whom graph point at the same Atlanta-Morehouse cohort rather than diverging. On the Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing axis his normalized position of +0.52 places him 12th of 136 audited entries from the sacred end, well onto the Sacred Framing side, which tracks the church-anchored organizing tradition of the 1960 Atlanta sit-ins (his grandfather a Baptist evangelist, the manifesto-and-action sequence counseled by Morehouse and Atlanta University presidents who were themselves clergy and church-tied administrators). The embedding does not register the secular vocabulary of his later electoral and organizational career (NAACP chapter presidency, school-superintendent advocacy) as the dominant signal; the early church-based protest framing carries the vector instead.
biographical_paragraph: Lonnie C. King launched the Atlanta Student Movement in early 1960 and directed the production of the Appeal for Human Rights, the published statement that opened the Atlanta movement's account of segregation in the city; he appointed Roslyn Pope to draft and edit the text and assigned Julian Bond and Charles A. Black to the writing committee [src: 5, 8]. The Committee on Appeal for Human Rights that King chaired coordinated the sit-ins and the downtown boycott centered on Rich's department store [src: 5]. He later served as the Atlanta NAACP chapter president and advocated for the hiring of the city's first African American school superintendent [src: 2]. King was born in 1936 in Arlington, Georgia, and died March 5, 2019 [src: 2, 3]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 80 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Lonnie C. King
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked to pinpoint his beginnings, King describes growing up in rural Arlington, Georgia, raised by his grandfather, a Baptist evangelist:
interview_snippets.quote: I guess Dr. Emilye Crosby, if I had to pinpoint my beginnings, I have to say I believe that I was born looking up at the bottom.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lonnie C. King
interview_snippets.lead_in: King recalls his grandfather, who recruited NAACP memberships in southwest Georgia for fifty cents, explaining the organization to him as a child:
interview_snippets.quote: He said, son, one day, this organization's going to set us free. He said, they've got lawyers in New York and so forth and so on.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lonnie C. King
interview_snippets.lead_in: Around April 1960, King was asked to argue against returning to class in a Spelman debate opposite NAACP executive secretary Roy Wilkins; he closes his rebuttal:
interview_snippets.quote: And I ended up saying, Mr. Wilkins, if not now, when, and if not us, who? And I sat down.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lonnie C. King
interview_snippets.lead_in: King describes the briefing he gave students before the May 17, 1960 march, framing nonviolence as the movement's method rather than a personal creed:
interview_snippets.quote: I'm not asking you to be nonviolent in your life, just as a tactic.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lonnie C. King
interview_snippets.lead_in: Summoned by police chief Herbert Jenkins to Rich's department store owner Richard Rich in June 1960, King answers Rich's threat to jail him:
interview_snippets.quote: And I said, Mr. Rich, I will be back in the fall when I come back and bring thousands with me.

interview_snippets.speaker: Amos C. Brown
interview_snippets.lead_in: Amos C. Brown, the youngest member of the committee that produced the Appeal for Human Rights, lists who was in the drafting room and names King first:
interview_snippets.quote: The other persons in the room were Lonnie King, who still alive. Charles Black, who still alive. Herschelle Sullivan, Julian Bond, and Mary and Wright Elliman, it's Don Klaug, who's a Klaug, A.U. among others.

movement_context: The February 1, 1960 sit-in at the Woolworth lunch counter in Greensboro, North Carolina, reached Atlanta within five weeks through the campus communication network linking historically Black colleges across the South [src: 4]. Lonnie King, then a Morehouse College student and former U.S. Navy seaman, read the Greensboro accounts in the Atlanta Daily World and recognized that the Atlanta University Center, the consortium of Morehouse, Spelman, Clark, Morris Brown, the Interdenominational Theological Center, and Atlanta University, was an organizing substrate the Greensboro Four had not had access to [src: 5]. King and Joseph Pierce convened the initial Morehouse student meeting; the planning expanded to include Roslyn Pope of Spelman, Julian Bond of Morehouse, James Felder of Clark, and Marian Wright (later Marian Wright Edelman) of Spelman, the latter five drawn from the institutional leadership across the consortium campuses [src: 5]. Atlanta University president Rufus E. Clement and Morehouse president Benjamin E. Mays counseled the students to publish a manifesto before any direct action, so that the city's white press and political establishment could not characterize the protests as spontaneous lawlessness [src: 4]. Roslyn Pope drafted the text; Lonnie King, Julian Bond, and the broader student committee revised it; the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights paid for full-page placement, and on March 9, 1960 the manifesto ran in the Atlanta Constitution, the Atlanta Journal, and the Atlanta Daily World under the title 'An Appeal for Human Rights' [src: 4]. Six days later, on March 15, 1960, the first coordinated Atlanta sit-ins targeted Rich's Department Store, the Terminal Station coffee shop, the Trailways and Greyhound bus station cafeterias, the State Capitol cafeteria, and the federal-government cafeterias in the Sloan Building and the Citizens Trust Building [src: 5]. The arrests that followed were the first mass sit-in arrests of Atlanta college students and marked the entrance of the Atlanta University Center cohort into the post-Greensboro southern student protest movement [src: 4]. The following month, on April 15-17, 1960, the SCLC convened roughly two hundred student delegates from across the South at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina; King attended as one of the Atlanta delegates, and the gathering founded the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee [src: 6]. The Atlanta Student Movement's institutional posture, the consortium-coordinated manifesto-then-action sequence, the press-grade documentation, and the multi-target opening day, became one of the templates the new SNCC field staff carried into other southern cities through 1960-1962 [src: 6].
legacy_and_reception: After the 1960-1961 Atlanta campaign King left the movement's day-to-day organizing for graduate study, completing a Master of Education and a Juris Doctor at the University of Missouri while working as a federal civil-rights compliance officer; he returned to Atlanta in the 1980s and was elected to the Atlanta School Board, where he served through the 1990s [src: 2]. He took the presidency of the Atlanta NAACP branch in the 2000s and led the chapter's campaign for the hiring of Beverly L. Hall as the city's first African American school superintendent, the appointment that drew him back into Atlanta civic politics for the last decade of his life [src: 2]. King died March 5, 2019 in Atlanta at age eighty-two [src: 3]. On the seventy-fifth anniversary of his birth, the Atlanta City Council renamed the stretch of Fair Street SW that runs through the Atlanta University Center the Lonnie C. King Jr. Atlanta Student Movement Boulevard, recognizing his role as the Atlanta Student Movement's founding chair and the route's adjacency to Morehouse, Spelman, Clark Atlanta, and the Robert W. Woodruff Library [src: 2]. His papers and oral-history record are held in the Library of Congress American Folklife Center's Civil Rights History Project accession AFC 2010/039 [src: 1] and discussed in the institutional histories of the Atlanta sit-in movement [src: 5] and the SNCC founding period [src: 6].

gallery.alt: Graves Hall on the Morehouse College campus, Atlanta, Georgia
gallery.caption: Graves Hall on the Morehouse College campus in Atlanta, the oldest building on Morehouse's main quadrangle. Lonnie King was a Morehouse student in early 1960 when he convened the initial planning meeting that became the Atlanta Student Movement; Morehouse and the broader Atlanta University Center consortium were the institutional substrate from which the March 9, 1960 'An Appeal for Human Rights' and the March 15, 1960 sit-ins emerged.
gallery.license: Creative Commons CC0 1.0 Universal Public Domain Dedication

gallery.alt: Martin Luther King Jr. at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina, April 1960
gallery.caption: Martin Luther King Jr. at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina, photographed April 16, 1960. The image is from the SCLC-convened student conference April 15-17, 1960 at which the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee was founded; Lonnie King attended as one of the Atlanta Student Movement delegates, six weeks after the Atlanta sit-ins he had co-organized began.
gallery.photographer: State Archives of North Carolina (Raleigh, NC)

gallery.alt: Rich's Downtown Department Store, 45 Broad Street, Atlanta, view of east side of the 1924 store
gallery.caption: Rich's Downtown Department Store at 45 Broad Street in Atlanta, Historic American Buildings Survey documentation of the east side of the 1924 store. Rich's segregated Magnolia Room restaurant was the primary target of the March 15, 1960 Atlanta sit-ins Lonnie King and the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights coordinated; the boycott of Rich's was the longest-running of the Atlanta sit-in actions and was a leverage point in the March 6, 1961 desegregation settlement.
gallery.photographer: Historic American Buildings Survey (U.S. National Park Service)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from the Library of Congress HABS collection)
gallery.license: Public domain (work of the U.S. federal government, 17 U.S.C. § 105)
